narrative:
Sunday shattered record highs for the date, but a front moves through from the north this morning. A shower or two develops this morning, mostly on the west side of the Appalachians (a rumble of thunder is not impossible); then cooler air arrives on a breezy NW wind. We return to the reality of seasonal temperatures for late March Tuesday. Temperatures this week roll in waves of warm and chilly... warmer Thursday, then colder Saturday. This “rolling wave” pattern continues as far as the weather eye can see.

Is winter over? This week's Monday Message tackles this question.

Turning Cooler

Last Updated Mon March 23 at 10:57AM by Ray Russell

Sunday shattered record highs for the date, but a front moves through from the north this morning. A shower or two develops this morning, mostly on the west side of the Appalachians (a rumble of thunder is not impossible); then cooler air arrives on a breezy NW wind. We return to the reality of seasonal temperatures for late March Tuesday. Temperatures this week roll in waves of warm and chilly... warmer Thursday, then colder Saturday. This “rolling wave” pattern continues as far as the weather eye can see.

Is winter over? This week's Monday Message tackles this question.

Forecast Discussion

We shattered high temperature records Sunday. As an example, it will take a day or two for all the official data to come in, but it may be the earliest date of an 80-degree high ever recorded in Boone, NC.

A front moves through this morning. A few showers are possible this morning, mostly on the west side of the Appalachians. A rumble of thunder is not impossible. Temperatures peak around sunrise, then gradually cool through the rest of the day, leading to a chilly Monday night. Tuesday is about 30 degrees cooler than Sunday—quite a contrast.

High pressure moves from the Ohio Valley Tuesday to off the New Jersey coast Wednesday. The cool air begins to erode Wednesday afternoon and Wednesday night. Thursday is much warmer.

The next cold front arrives Friday. Exact timing is still uncertain. We start the day warm. Showers and thundershowers develop, and much colder air arrives Friday night. Precipitation could end Friday evening as snowflakes on mountaintops.

As high pressure moves from Missouri to the Central Appalachians over the weekend, Saturday is cold (not extreme), with a chilly afternoon. Frost is a decent bet Saturday night and maybe in valleys Sunday night. Temperatures gradually moderate early next week.

Detailed forecast discussion and analysis is only available to RaysWeather supporters.
